chunks:
Proposal: A Hybrid Short–Long–Short Jump Scheme
Combine Dalzell's extended-phase anchor with Hastings' localized short-path anchor, then test whether the middle overlap is easier than the direct one.
Core thesis
Use two genuine anchors on opposite sides of the phase transition:
• ψ_D for the extended side
• ψ_H for the localized side
so the main cost becomes 1 / |⟨ψ_D|ψ_H⟩| instead of 1 / |⟨z*|ψ_D⟩|.
Dalzell et al. (2023)
Hastings (2018)
Research motivation: both papers avoid the bad gap region — but from opposite sides.
Can their two anchor states be stitched into one better pathway?
Papers used: Dalzell et al., “Mind the Gap: Achieving a Super-Grover Quantum Speedup by Jumping to the End” (STOC 2023); Hastings, “A Short Path Quantum Algorithm for Exact Optimization” (Quantum 2, 78, 2018).

Why a naive extra midpoint does not help
The midpoint must be a genuine anchor state, not just a filtered version of ψ_D.
Direct long jump
Fake midpoint
Real anchor midpoint
ψ_D  →  z*
If |⟨z*|ψ_D⟩| = 0.01,
then cost ≈ 1 / 0.01 = 100.
This direct jump is the bottleneck.
ψ_D  →  φ  →  z*
Take φ = Πψ_D / ||Πψ_D||.
If 0.01 = 0.1 × 0.1, the two pieces look easier,
but φ is not independently accessible.
So the effective cost still behaves like 10 × 10 = 100.
ψ_D  →  φ  →  z*
Now let φ be the unique gapped ground state
of an accessible Hamiltonian H_φ.
Then reflections about φ are available directly,
so the two jump costs can add.
Example: 10 + 10 = 20, genuinely better than 100.
Key point: one small overlap controls the expensive step.
Key point: splitting a projection is a wash.
Key point: find a true anchor, not a filter.
Context: Dalzell et al. jump framework and Hastings-style short-path grounding motivate the distinction between a real anchor state and a merely projected midpoint.

Proposed hybrid pathway
A short–long–short chain that uses one anchor from each paper.
Hastings anchor
Localized side of the transition
Gap argument via low-energy density assumption
Dalzell anchor
Extended side of the transition
Gap argument via tail bound
main bottleneck
cheap
cheap
|+⟩
ψ_D
ψ_H
|z*⟩
Research hypothesis
Why it might help
ψ_H collects amplitude from a neighborhood around z*, not only from the single optimum string.
So the middle overlap can benefit from constructive support over many nearby deep-valley states.
If |⟨ψ_D|ψ_H⟩| decays exponentially slower than |⟨z*|ψ_D⟩|, then replacing the direct long jump by a bridge between the two anchors should improve the exponent.
Anchor sources: Dalzell et al. (STOC 2023) for ψ_D and Hastings (Quantum 2, 78, 2018) for ψ_H. This slide proposes their composition as a new research direction.

Cheap go/no-go experiment
Before proving anything, test whether the middle overlap is empirically better on small instances.
1. Generate many small 3-spin / MAX-E-D-LIN2 instances (for example n ≈ 14–22).
Decision rule
If B(n) decays slower than A(n), the idea is alive.
If B(n) does not beat A(n), the proposal dies cheaply.
2. Exact diagonalize the Dalzell-style and Hastings-style Hamiltonians for each instance.
Success criterion
3. Compute two overlaps:  A(n) = |⟨z*|ψ_D⟩|   and   B(n) = |⟨ψ_D|ψ_H⟩|.
median slope of log₂ B(n)  >  median slope of log₂ A(n)
Interpretation
A positive gap here means the hybrid bridge is empirically easier than the direct Dalzell-to-optimum jump, which is enough to justify deeper theory and larger benchmarking.
4. Fit exponential slopes across many instances and compare the medians.
This deck proposes a testable research question, not a theorem.
Motivation for combination: Dalzell et al. conclude that their speedup mechanism may be worth combining with other techniques; the hybrid Dalzell–Hastings bridge is a natural quantum-side candidate.

References
Core papers behind the proposed hybrid pathway.
[1] Dalzell, A. M., Pancotti, N., Campbell, E. T., and Brandão, F. G. S. L.
Mind the Gap: Achieving a Super-Grover Quantum Speedup by Jumping to the End.
Proceedings of the 55th Annual ACM Symposium on Theory of Computing (STOC ’23), 2023.
arXiv:2212.01513.
[2] Hastings, M. B.
A Short Path Quantum Algorithm for Exact Optimization.
Quantum 2, 78 (2018). DOI: 10.22331/q-2018-07-26-78.
arXiv:1802.10124.
Proposed question for next work: can a Dalzell anchor and a Hastings anchor be chained so that the middle overlap exponent beats the direct Dalzell-to-optimum overlap exponent?
Reference metadata taken from the papers themselves: Dalzell et al. arXiv/ACM STOC 2023; Hastings, Quantum 2, 78 (2018).
